steps.title: Identify the paradox that must be resolved
steps.content: CLPs are shielded from the external environment during transmission (ingested by another host), while directly transmitted parasites are exposed to external conditions. Despite this apparent advantage, three-host CLP abundance declined as temperatures rose, while directly transmitted parasites remained largely stable. The blank must explain how shielded parasites still declined.

steps.title: Eliminate each wrong choice and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• "as the number of host species involved in a parasite's transmission increases, the parasite is better protected against rising temperatures.": Contradicts the data — CLPs with three hosts actually declined more than directly transmitted parasites, so more hosts did not confer better protection. ✗
  • "directly transmitted parasites identified in the study were more likely to use transmission strategies that shield them from warming temperatures than were three-host CLPs.": Inverts the passage — the text explicitly states CLPs are shielded during transmission while directly transmitted parasites are exposed. ✗
  • "CLPs primarily transmitted by ingestion were less dependent on host species adversely affected by warming temperatures than were CLPs that use other transmission strategies.": Introduces a distinction among CLP transmission strategies that the passage does not make and does not explain the main finding. ✗
  • "any advantages that the transmission strategy used by three-host CLPs may have conferred did not completely offset the negative effects of other temperature-driven factors on CLP abundance.": Resolves the paradox directly — the shielding advantage existed but was outweighed by other temperature-driven harms, explaining why CLPs still declined despite their protected transmission. ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
